title: Painting a feature wall in chalk paint
subtitle: Apply a flat, matte chalk finish to one wall with minimal preparation
excerpt: How to paint a single wall in chalk paint for a flat, powdery matte finish. Covers the distinctive surface preparation (chalk paint adheres to most surfaces without sanding), applying two coats with a large brush, and finishing with a clear wax or matte varnish to protect the surface.
sourceNotes: BS 6150: Painting of buildings. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. Victorian-era distemper and chalk paint traditions documented in Eastlake's Hints on Household Taste (1868).

glossaryTerms.definition: A water-based decorative paint with a very high chalk (calcium carbonate) content, giving a flat, powdery matte finish. Adheres to most surfaces — wood, plaster, brick, melamine — with minimal preparation. Dries quickly (30 to 60 minutes between coats). Requires a protective wax or varnish coat to resist scuffing.

A paste wax of carnauba or beeswax blended in a solvent carrier, used as a topcoat over chalk paint. Applied in a thin circular motion and buffed to a satin sheen. Protects the chalk surface from moisture and everyday scuffing; re-wax annually in high-traffic areas.

content.text: Chalk paint's main appeal is that it bonds to almost any reasonably clean surface without a primer or sanding. On a plain plaster or painted wall it is a straightforward process: two coats with a wide brush, a wax topcoat, and done. The result is a soft, powdery finish quite different from a standard emulsion wall.
is thicker than emulsion and tends to show brushmarks, which most people find attractive; a roller gives a smoother surface but loses some of the handmade quality that distinguishes the finish.

Chalk paint in the chosen colour (500 ml covers approximately 5 to 7 m² in two coats). Clear furniture wax for the topcoat. A lint-free cloth for the wax application and buffing. Painter's masking tape for the adjacent walls, ceiling line, and skirting.

Wipe the wall down with a damp cloth to remove dust and loose dirt; allow to dry. Fill any obvious cracks or holes with filler and sand flat. A single light pass of 120-grit paper over any glossy patches on the wall will help adhesion, but chalk paint does not require a complete key across the whole surface. Mask the ceiling, adjacent walls, and skirting with painter's tape. Lay the cotton dust sheet to protect the floor. Check that the wall is not damp; chalk paint will not adhere to a wet or damp surface.

Stir the chalk paint thoroughly; it settles quickly. Work from the top of the wall downward in large, loose strokes with the 50 mm brush. Chalk paint is not self-levelling, so work quickly across the whole wall in one session to avoid lap marks where dried edges meet fresh paint. The first coat will look patchy; this is normal. The high chalk content means the paint is quite opaque but the first coat rarely covers perfectly. Allow to dry fully, 30 to 60 minutes at room temperature.

Apply the second coat in the same way, working from the top down. The second coat should give even coverage. Where the wall has a highly textured finish (heavy artex or a coarse sand texture), a third coat may be needed to fill the hollows. The brushmarks from the second coat are the final appearance; if you prefer a smoother surface, stipple or smooth the second coat with the roller instead of a brush.
Step 4, apply the protective wax topcoat
Once the second coat is fully dry (allow 2 hours before waxing), apply the

with a lint-free cloth in small circular strokes, working in sections of approximately 300 × 300 mm. Apply a very thin coat; thick wax takes much longer to buff and can go streaky. Allow the wax to haze for 5 to 10 minutes, then buff with a clean dry cloth until the surface feels smooth rather than tacky. For a feature wall on a low-traffic surface (a bedroom head wall), one wax coat is sufficient. For a hall wall or anywhere hands touch the surface, apply two coats.

A matte varnish can replace the wax on high-traffic walls where re-waxing is inconvenient. It dries harder and requires no buffing but gives a slightly flatter sheen than wax. Two coats of matte varnish over chalk paint gives a washable surface.

Chalk paint can be diluted with up to 30% water for a washed, semi-transparent effect. Apply with a broad brush and wipe back partially with a damp cloth before drying; the result is a colour-wash that shows the wall texture beneath.

attrs.symptom: Paint is peeling away from the wall within days of application
attrs.cause: The wall surface was damp or had a flaking existing coat that was not removed first.
attrs.fix: Strip the chalk paint. Resolve the damp issue if present (rising damp, condensation). Remove all flaking existing paint, allow the wall to dry, and re-apply.

attrs.symptom: Visible lap marks where sections of the wet paint met dried edges
attrs.cause: The paint dried too quickly; each section was allowed to dry before the adjacent section was applied.
attrs.fix: Apply a dilute wash coat (chalk paint diluted to 70% with water) over the entire wall to blend the lap marks, then re-apply the full-strength final coat in one session, working quickly across the whole wall.

attrs.symptom: Chalky marks appearing on clothing that brushes the wall
attrs.cause: No protective wax or varnish coat applied; bare chalk paint marks easily.
attrs.fix: Apply the clear wax topcoat to the affected areas, allow to haze, and buff well.
